addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

# Todo

This file uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It
contains near term tasks with a short description and
uses links or reference links for more details.

## In Progress

_No cycle currently in progress._

## Todo

 Entries are in **strict priority rank** — #1 highest,
 descending. Reprioritize by moving an entry, then
 `vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run notes/todo.md` to renumber.
 The numbers are positional rank, not stable IDs — to refer
 to a Todo, name it by its **title** (a greppable mention;
 a numbered list item has no anchor to link to), not its
 number. Long-tail entries
 live in [todo-backlog.md](todo-backlog.md). Use the
 [Prose Form in AGENTS.md](../AGENTS.md#prose-form); deeper
 detail goes in `notes/chores/chores-NN.md` design
 subsections (link via `[N]` ref).

1. Descriptor queue endpoints: paired DescSender (loan +
   send) / DescReceiver (recv) [[11]]:
   - own ring endpoint + registry access;
   - the demo's ~20-line send path becomes ~3 lines;
   - `resolve`'s unsafe is audited once inside the crate
     (recv safe by construction);
   - guard handed back on Full;
   - design against both ring flavors (SPSC + MPSC);
   - the sender is also where each sender's private
     overflow pending list will live.
2. Overflow FIFO: on ring Full, append the message to a
   sender-private pending list instead of failing
   [details](ring-buffer-design.md#overflow-fifo-future):
   - intrusive — the same embedded next-link the
     free-stack uses, so zero allocation;
   - naturally bounded by pool capacity;
   - composes per-sender with MPSC — see
     [Overflow readiness](ring-buffer-design.md#overflow-readiness).
3. Explore why zcr-mpsc-2t measures faster than zcr-with-2t
   (73.9 vs 100.1 ns adjusted mean at 300s, 1p/1c) and
   whether the mechanism can improve SPSC [[20]]:
   - we think SPSC bounces two index lines per handoff
     (each side polls the line the other writes) while
     MPSC's only shared hot word is the slot seq;
   - verify with perf cache-transfer counters and/or the
     padded-seq variant (a design open question);
   - if confirmed, a seam-word variant might feed back
     into the SPSC protocol.
4. Batch alloc/free demo: alongside the one-message
   alloc_free_1t loops, a variant that allocs X messages
   (5, 10, …) then frees them all, pool vs global
   allocator. We think the pool's rate stays constant
   (pop/push is O(1) regardless of live count, LIFO keeps
   the working set hot) while Box::new/drop slows as the
   batch outgrows malloc's thread-cache fast path — the
   demo should show it.

content.text: Now let me read the SPSC side to ground the contrast.

Read the full picture. Here's the education, grounded in the code.

## The SPSC baseline: two single-owner indices

The SPSC ring (`src/producer.rs`, `src/consumer.rs`) has two `AtomicU32` counters in the header, and the whole protocol rests on **each being written by exactly one side**:

- `producer_idx` — free-running count of slots *reserved*. Producer writes it (Release, on `commit`, `producer.rs:167`); consumer only reads it (Acquire, to check non-empty, `consumer.rs:90`).
- `consumer_idx` — free-running count of slots *released*. Consumer writes it (Release, `consumer.rs:141`); producer only reads it (Acquire, to check non-full, `producer.rs:104`).

Because each counter has a single writer, neither side needs a CAS. The producer *owns* `producer_idx` outright — it loads it once (`producer.rs:98`, "Ours alone: no peer moves it") and just stores `p+1` to publish. Occupancy is `p - c`.

That single-writer assumption is exactly what a second producer would break: two producers both read `p`, both write slot `p`, both store `p+1` — one message is silently lost. SPSC can't grow a producer without changing the protocol.

## What MPSC changes to admit many producers

Two structural changes (`src/mpsc/`):

**1. `producer_idx` becomes a CAS-claimed ticket counter.** A producer no longer owns it. It reads `pos`, and to claim that slot it must win a `compare_exchange_weak(pos, pos+1)` (`mpsc/producer.rs:145`). Winner gets exclusive use of slot `pos`; losers get the fresher `pos` back and retry. That CAS is the serialization point among producers — it's the *only* thing producers contend on with each other, and it's why the module raises the atomics floor to `target_has_atomic="32"` (`mpsc/mod.rs:16`).

**2. A per-slot `seq` array replaces "read the other side's index" for publication.** This is the subtler half. Under concurrent producers, *claim order and commit order differ* — producer A can win slot 5 but still be filling it when producer B finishes slot 6. So a single "producer_idx = how many are committed" counter no longer works; slot 6 being done doesn't mean slot 5 is. Each slot gets its own publication word, `seq[pos]` (`mpsc/mod.rs:113`), seeded to `seq[i]=i` at init (`mpsc/mod.rs:165-169`). The lifecycle of one slot's seq word:

- **claimable** at position `pos` when `seq[pos] == pos` (`producer.rs:141-142`, `diff == 0`).
- **committed / readable** when the producer stores `seq[pos] = pos+1` (Release, `producer.rs:190`). The consumer sees it when `seq[c] == c+1` (`consumer.rs:104-105`).
- **released / claimable next lap** when the consumer stores `seq[c] = c+capacity` (Release, `consumer.rs:182`) — i.e. the position that will next reach this slot, one lap later.

**Fullness now comes from the seq, not from `consumer_idx`.** A producer that finds `seq[pos] - pos < 0` (`producer.rs:154`) knows the slot's previous occupant hasn't been released yet — that's "full" — *without ever reading `consumer_idx`*. Symmetrically the consumer never reads `producer_idx`; it reads its own `consumer_idx` and the seq. There are two extra wrinkles the seq encoding buys:

- **Tombstones** (`TOMBSTONE = 1<<31`, `mod.rs:56`) — if a producer's fill closure *panics*, an abandoned claim would wedge the queue forever (the consumer waits at that slot). The `TombstoneOnUnwind` guard (`producer.rs:54-71`) instead commits `pos+1+TOMBSTONE`; the consumer recognizes that exact value (`consumer.rs:108`) and releases the slot without delivering. This is why capacity is capped at `2^30` not `2^31` (`mod.rs:44`) — headroom so the tombstone offset stays unambiguous.
- **Stale-claim detection** — a producer seeing `diff < 0` re-reads `producer_idx` first (`producer.rs:159`); if it moved, this was a tombstoned/raced slot, not a genuine full ring, so no policy call.

The consumer stays **completely CAS-free** (`mpsc/consumer.rs:14`) — single consumer, so it just walks `consumer_idx` forward.

## Why this sets up question #3

Here's the crux that makes MPSC-2t beat SPSC-2t, and it falls right out of the above. Count the cache lines that **bounce between the producer core and consumer core** per message:

**SPSC:** *both* index lines cross-bounce every handoff.
- `producer_idx`: producer writes → consumer reads → line transfers producer→consumer.
- `consumer_idx`: consumer writes → producer reads → line transfers consumer→producer.
- Two lines ping-ponging across the core boundary, each and every message.

**MPSC (at 1p/1c, so the claim CAS is uncontended):**
- `producer_idx` is now touched **only by the producer** (its own CAS; the consumer never reads it) → stays hot in the producer's L1, never bounces.
- `consumer_idx` is touched **only by the consumer** → stays in the consumer's L1, never bounces.
- The only word that crosses the boundary is the **slot seq** (producer commits it, consumer reads+releases it) → *one* bouncing line instead of two.

So MPSC, by making each index private and moving the producer↔consumer handshake onto the per-slot seq, **halves the cross-core coherence traffic** on the hot path — at the cost of the seq array (and, when there really are ≥2 producers, CAS contention on `producer_idx`, which the 1p case doesn't pay). That's the mechanism the todo hypothesizes ("SPSC bounces two index lines... MPSC's only shared hot word is the slot seq"), and it lines up with 73.9 vs 100.1 ns.

That's the theory. The open work in #3 is to **confirm it's actually the cache-line bounce** and not something else (branch layout, the CAS being cheaper than expected, etc.) — the todo names two ways: perf cache-transfer counters, and a padded-seq variant to see if seq-line packing matters.

Before I go further — how do you want to proceed on the verification? Options as I see them:

1. **perf counters** — run the existing 1p/1c benchmark under `perf stat` with cache-coherence events (`mem_load_l3_miss_retired.remote_fwd` / HITM-type counters) on both SPSC and MPSC, and compare cross-core transfers per message against the ~26 ns gap.
2. **padded-seq experiment** — one seq per cache line instead of 16-per-line, to isolate false-sharing-within-the-seq-array from the true index-bounce effect.
3. Just wanted the conceptual grounding for now, and we'll scope the experiment next.

Which direction?

688	## feat: mpsc ring sibling primitive
689	
690	Commits: [[37]],[[38]],[[39]],[[40]],[[41]],[[42]]
691	
692	N senders sharing one queue needs a multi-producer ring, and
693	the SPSC hot path (load/store-only, ~2 ns 1t) must not pay
694	for it — so MPSC lands as a separate sibling primitive,
695	measured A/B against SPSC in iiac-perf. Design:
696	[MPSC ring (sibling primitive)](../ring-buffer-design.md#mpsc-ring-sibling-primitive).
697	
698	- Vyukov's bounded MPMC restricted to MP/SC:
699	  - producers CAS-claim `producer_idx`;
700	  - a per-slot seq array publishes each slot independently
701	    (claim order ≠ commit order under concurrency);
702	  - the consumer stays CAS-free, and producers never read
703	    `consumer_idx` — fullness comes from the slot seq.
704	- Closure send (`send_with`) makes claim abandonment
705	  unrepresentable; a panicking fill publishes a tombstoned
706	  commit (`pos + 1 + 2^31`) the consumer releases without
707	  delivering — an unwind cannot wedge the queue.
708	- Own magic (`"ZCM1"`) and layout version; the module is
709	  gated on `target_has_atomic = "32"`. Filing the gate found
710	  bug #1: the pool's free-stack CAS is ungated, so
711	  load/store-only targets already fail to build.
712	- Demo grew mpsc lines at every placement plus the 2p+1c
713	  shape; iiac-perf grew `zcr-mpsc-1t`/`zcr-mpsc-2t` (bench
714	  code in the sibling repo, committed there separately).
715	- Deferred to the design's measurement plan: the 2p+1c
716	  contention bench and the fan-in comparator — the
717	  round-trip harness needs a competing-producer shape
718	  first.
719	
720	### Outcome: the 2t surprise
721	
722	300s adjusted means: `zcr-with-1t` 2.3 ns vs `zcr-mpsc-1t`
723	4.4 ns — the uncontended claim CAS + seq publish costs
724	~2.1 ns, as expected. Cross-thread at 1p/1c the sign flips:
725	`zcr-mpsc-2t` 73.9 ns vs `zcr-with-2t` 100.1 ns — the MPSC
726	protocol measures *faster*, with a wider spread (p10 35.8 /
727	p90 89.9 vs SPSC's tight 88–98). We think the SPSC
728	round-trip bounces two index cache lines per handoff (each
729	side polls the line the other writes) while the MPSC
730	handoff's only shared hot word is the slot seq — neither
731	side ever reads the other's index line. Promoted to a
732	`## Todo` entry (explore via perf cache-transfer counters
733	and/or the padded-seq variant; if confirmed, a seam-word
734	variant might feed back into the SPSC protocol).

## feat: mpsc ring sibling primitive

Commits: [[37]],[[38]],[[39]],[[40]],[[41]],[[42]]

N senders sharing one queue needs a multi-producer ring, and
the SPSC hot path (load/store-only, ~2 ns 1t) must not pay
for it — so MPSC lands as a separate sibling primitive,
measured A/B against SPSC in iiac-perf. Design:
[MPSC ring (sibling primitive)](../ring-buffer-design.md#mpsc-ring-sibling-primitive).

- Vyukov's bounded MPMC restricted to MP/SC:
  - producers CAS-claim `producer_idx`;
  - a per-slot seq array publishes each slot independently
    (claim order ≠ commit order under concurrency);
  - the consumer stays CAS-free, and producers never read
    `consumer_idx` — fullness comes from the slot seq.
- Closure send (`send_with`) makes claim abandonment
  unrepresentable; a panicking fill publishes a tombstoned
  commit (`pos + 1 + 2^31`) the consumer releases without
  delivering — an unwind cannot wedge the queue.
- Own magic (`"ZCM1"`) and layout version; the module is
  gated on `target_has_atomic = "32"`. Filing the gate found
  bug #1: the pool's free-stack CAS is ungated, so
  load/store-only targets already fail to build.
- Demo grew mpsc lines at every placement plus the 2p+1c
  shape; iiac-perf grew `zcr-mpsc-1t`/`zcr-mpsc-2t` (bench
  code in the sibling repo, committed there separately).
- Deferred to the design's measurement plan: the 2p+1c
  contention bench and the fan-in comparator — the
  round-trip harness needs a competing-producer shape
  first.

### Outcome: the 2t surprise

300s adjusted means: `zcr-with-1t` 2.3 ns vs `zcr-mpsc-1t`
4.4 ns — the uncontended claim CAS + seq publish costs
~2.1 ns, as expected. Cross-thread at 1p/1c the sign flips:
`zcr-mpsc-2t` 73.9 ns vs `zcr-with-2t` 100.1 ns — the MPSC
protocol measures *faster*, with a wider spread (p10 35.8 /
p90 89.9 vs SPSC's tight 88–98). We think the SPSC
round-trip bounces two index cache lines per handoff (each
side polls the line the other writes) while the MPSC
handoff's only shared hot word is the slot seq — neither
side ever reads the other's index line. Promoted to a
`## Todo` entry (explore via perf cache-transfer counters
and/or the padded-seq variant; if confirmed, a seam-word
variant might feed back into the SPSC protocol).

### As-built ladder

- `0.11.0-0` docs: mpsc ring design + plan
- `0.11.0-1` feat: mpsc ring layout + init/attach
- `0.11.0-2` feat: mpsc send_with + consumer recv
- `0.11.0-3` feat: demo mpsc flow lines
- `0.11.0-4` docs: mpsc iiac-perf numbers
- `0.11.0` feat: mpsc ring sibling primitive

# Notes

This directory contains various notes and documentation related to the project.
Each file is organized by topic for easy reference.

By default there are chores/chores-*.md and todo.md. Chores are general notes
about tasks and todo.md contains short term tasks and their status.
The chores-NN files are numbered in sequence; as a chores file grows
large the next one is opened ([chores/chores-01.md](chores/chores-01.md)
is the active one), and earlier ones are closed and
kept for history.

Project design docs:

- [ring-buffer-design.md](ring-buffer-design.md) — the
  zero-copy ring buffer and its MPSC sibling (terminology,
  requirements, layout, API, validation), kept in sync with
  `src/`.


Examples chore file:
```
# Chores-01.md
 
General maintenance tasks and considerations for the project see other files for
more specific topics. A chore in a chores file provides quick information on the
how and why of a particular chore.

## Create a binary that lists jj info 

This binary should list the changeID, commitID, and description title
and using `jj-lib`
```

## Workflow and conventions

Bot-facing workflow and conventions live in
[`../AGENTS.md`](../AGENTS.md):

- [Notes file conventions](../AGENTS.md#notes-file-conventions)
  — Todo format, Reference numbering, Notes references
  (`[[N]]` citation style), Markdown anchor links, Retiring
  Done entries.
- [Chores conventions](../AGENTS.md#chores-conventions) —
  section headers / Done entries exact-title rule, content
  rules, `Commits:` line format.
- [Code Conventions](../AGENTS.md#code-conventions) — doc
  comments, `// OK: …` on `unwrap*` calls, ask-on-ambiguity,
  stuck detection.

Per-cycle workflow lives in
[`cycle-protocol.md`](cycle-protocol.md):

- [Cycles](cycle-protocol.md#cycles) — three-phase shape
  (Preparation → Work → Close-out), `X.Y.Z-N` numbering,
  sub-cycles.
- [Per-commit flow](cycle-protocol.md#per-commit-flow) —
  cargo cycle (`fmt` / `clippy` / `test` / `install`),
  work + commit description review gates.
- [Commit description](cycle-protocol.md#commit-description)
  — Conventional Commits title rules (no version suffix,
  distinct per step); body shape per app vs `.claude` repo.
- [Pushing](cycle-protocol.md#pushing) — push policy,
  close-out shape, `.claude` cadence.

This repo's versioning lives in
[`versioning.md`](versioning.md) — generic and shared
verbatim; the single source of truth that AGENTS.md and
cycle-protocol.md refer to abstractly:

- [Terms](versioning.md#terms) — version / version-of-record /

### As-built ladder
input.new_string:
variant might feed back into the SPSC protocol).

The mechanism sharpened — a per-message count of the cache
lines that cross the producer↔consumer core boundary. Both
rings carry two shared index words plus the slots; what
differs is *who reads whom*:

- SPSC bounces both index lines every handoff:
  - `producer_idx` — producer writes it on commit
    (`producer.rs` `commit`), consumer reads it on the
    non-empty check (`consumer.rs` reserve loop) → the line
    transfers producer→consumer.
  - `consumer_idx` — consumer writes it on release,
    producer reads it on the non-full check → the line
    transfers consumer→producer.
- MPSC at 1p/1c (the claim CAS uncontended) bounces one:
  - `producer_idx` is touched *only* by the producer (its
    claim CAS; the consumer never reads it) → stays hot in
    the producer's L1.
  - `consumer_idx` is touched *only* by the consumer
    (its own resume state; producers read fullness from the
    seq) → stays hot in the consumer's L1.
  - only the slot `seq` crosses — the producer commits it
    (`seq[pos] = pos+1`, Release), the consumer reads then
    releases it (`seq[c] = c+capacity`, Release).

We think that halving — two bouncing lines down to one — is
the ~26 ns gap. What MPSC trades for it: the seq array's
footprint, and, once there are ≥2 producers, CAS contention
on `producer_idx` that the 1p case never pays. The
per-slot-independent seq is *forced* by concurrency (claim
order ≠ commit order), but its side effect at 1p/1c is
retiring one of SPSC's two cross-core reads — which is why
a seam-word SPSC variant (give SPSC a seq-like publish word
so neither side reads the other's index) is the feedback
path worth testing.

# Chores-02

Chores-XX files use [Prose form](../../AGENTS.md#prose-form). They
contain discussions and notes on various chores in github compatible
markdown. There is also a [todo.md](../todo.md) file that tracks
tasks and in general there should be a chore section for each task
with the why and how this task will be completed.

Continues [chores-01.md](chores-01.md), opened as that file
approached ~1000 lines. Reference numbering restarts at `[1]`
(file-local — see
[Reference numbering](../../AGENTS.md#reference-numbering)).

## refactor: sibling module dirs (spsc, mpsc, pool)

Commits:

The three ring/pool primitives sit at uneven depths: `mpsc/`
is already a directory, but the SPSC ring is spread across
top-level `src/producer.rs` / `src/consumer.rs` plus its
core types in `src/lib.rs`, and the pool is a single
`src/pool.rs`. Give SPSC and pool their own directories so
all three read as siblings and `src/lib.rs` narrows to the
genuinely shared core.

Provisional plan (rough — the ladder is nailed down with the
user before the cycle opens):

- `src/spsc/` — move `producer.rs`, `consumer.rs`, and the
  SPSC-specific core out of `lib.rs`: `Header`, `Ring`,
  `MAGIC`, `LAYOUT_VERSION`, `header_ptr`, `region_size`,
  `validate_geometry`.
- `src/pool/` — promote `pool.rs` to `pool/mod.rs` (split
  further only if a real seam appears).
- `src/lib.rs` — keeps only what all three share:
  `CacheAligned`, `CACHE_LINE_SIZE`, `USER_WORDS`, `Error`,
  `check_type` / `type_fits`, `slot_ptr`; plus the crate's
  public re-exports.

### Open design questions

Settle these with the user before writing the ladder:

- **Public name surface** — keep the flat re-exports
  (`Producer`, `Consumer`, `Ring`) for source compat, or
  namespace them (`spsc::Ring`, `mpsc::MpscRing`)? Namespacing
  would let the `Mpsc*` prefixes drop, at the cost of a
  breaking rename.
- **Where `registry` lives** — the descriptor registry is
  SPSC-flavored today; does it move under `spsc/`, stay
  top-level as cross-cutting, or wait for the descriptor-queue
  endpoints work (Todo #1)?
- **Shared core home** — leave the shared bits inline in
  `lib.rs`, or lift them into a `core`/`common` submodule so
  `lib.rs` is purely wiring + re-exports?
- **Pool as a directory now vs later** — `pool.rs` is one
  file with no internal seam yet; a bare `pool/mod.rs` buys
  symmetry but nothing structural. Worth it, or leave pool a
  file until it actually splits?

# References

# Notes

This directory contains various notes and documentation related to the project.
Each file is organized by topic for easy reference.

By default there are chores/chores-*.md and todo.md. Chores are general notes
about tasks and todo.md contains short term tasks and their status.
The chores-NN files are numbered in sequence; as a chores file grows
large the next one is opened ([chores/chores-01.md](chores/chores-01.md)
is the active one), and earlier ones are closed and
kept for history.

Project design docs:

- [ring-buffer-design.md](ring-buffer-design.md) — the
  zero-copy ring buffer and its MPSC sibling (terminology,
  requirements, layout, API, validation), kept in sync with
  `src/`.


Examples chore file:
```
# Chores-01.md
 
General maintenance tasks and considerations for the project see other files for
more specific topics. A chore in a chores file provides quick information on the
how and why of a particular chore.

## Create a binary that lists jj info 

This binary should list the changeID, commitID, and description title
and using `jj-lib`
```

## Workflow and conventions

Bot-facing workflow and conventions live in
[`../AGENTS.md`](../AGENTS.md):

- [Notes file conventions](../AGENTS.md#notes-file-conventions)
  — Todo format, Reference numbering, Notes references
  (`[[N]]` citation style), Markdown anchor links, Retiring
  Done entries.
- [Chores conventions](../AGENTS.md#chores-conventions) —
  section headers / Done entries exact-title rule, content
  rules, `Commits:` line format.
- [Code Conventions](../AGENTS.md#code-conventions) — doc
  comments, `// OK: …` on `unwrap*` calls, ask-on-ambiguity,
  stuck detection.

Per-cycle workflow lives in
[`cycle-protocol.md`](cycle-protocol.md):

- [Cycles](cycle-protocol.md#cycles) — three-phase shape
  (Preparation → Work → Close-out), `X.Y.Z-N` numbering,
  sub-cycles.
- [Per-commit flow](cycle-protocol.md#per-commit-flow) —
  cargo cycle (`fmt` / `clippy` / `test` / `install`),
  work + commit description review gates.
- [Commit description](cycle-protocol.md#commit-description)
  — Conventional Commits title rules (no version suffix,
  distinct per step); body shape per app vs `.claude` repo.
- [Pushing](cycle-protocol.md#pushing) — push policy,
  close-out shape, `.claude` cadence.

This repo's versioning lives in
[`versioning.md`](versioning.md) — generic and shared
verbatim; the single source of truth that AGENTS.md and
cycle-protocol.md refer to abstractly:

- [Terms](versioning.md#terms) — version / version-of-record /
  versioning.
- [Recording the version-of-record](versioning.md#recording-the-version-of-record)
  — manifest, notation, reporter, and cadence, by medium.
- [Step numbering](versioning.md#step-numbering) — the
  `X.Y.Z-N` scheme, nesting, optional Preparation.

359	## MPSC ring (sibling primitive)
360	
361	Design for the multi-producer single-consumer ring — a
362	sibling type next to the SPSC ring, not a mode of it. The
363	SPSC hot path is one Relaxed load + one Acquire load + one
364	Release store per side; any unified type would put at least a
365	per-slot sequence check into that path. So the primitives
366	stay separate, existing SPSC users pay nothing, and iiac-perf
367	measures the two directly against each other. Anticipated as
368	a one-bullet placeholder in
369	[Pool topology and phasing](#pool-topology-and-phasing); this
370	section is the full design.
371	
372	### MPSC protocol
373	
374	Vyukov's bounded MPMC queue restricted to many-producer /
375	one-consumer: producers CAS-claim `producer_idx`; a per-slot
376	sequence word publishes each slot independently. The seq
377	exists because claim order and commit order differ under
378	concurrency — the consumer must observe commits, not claims.
379	
380	- **Sequence array** — `M × AtomicU32`, `seq[i]` initialized
381	  to `i`. Slot at free-running position `pos` (masked at
382	  access, as SPSC) is:
383	  - claimable when `seq == pos`,
384	  - committed when `seq == pos + 1`,
385	  - released by the consumer storing `seq = pos + M`.
386	- **Producer claim** — load `producer_idx` (Relaxed), load
387	  that slot's seq (Acquire), branch on the wrapping-signed
388	  diff `seq.wrapping_sub(pos) as i32`:
389	  - `== 0` — claimable:
390	    - `compare_exchange_weak(pos, pos + 1)` on
391	      `producer_idx`; success claims the slot exclusively.
392	    - Write the payload in place.
393	    - `seq.store(pos + 1, Release)` commits.
394	  - `< 0` — full:
395	    - The slot's previous occupant is not yet released.
396	    - The `on_full` policy decides retry vs give-up.
397	  - `> 0` — lost the race to another producer:
398	    - Reload `producer_idx` and retry.
399	    - Not a policy call: the system made progress, only
400	      this producer fell behind.
401	- **Consumer** — single, as in SPSC, so its side needs no
402	  CAS:
403	  - wait for `seq == c + 1` (Acquire),
404	  - read the slot,
405	  - store `seq = c + M` (Release),
406	  - advance the private index.
407	- **Producers never read `consumer_idx`**:
408	  - fullness falls out of the slot seq;
409	  - the header's consumer index line is diagnostic
410	    occupancy only.
411	- **Free-running u32 + power-of-two M** carry over
412	  unchanged: `2^32 % M == 0` keeps seq arithmetic and masked
413	  positions wrap-safe.
414	- **Atomic floor** — the claim CAS raises the floor for MPSC
415	  users only:
416	  - the module is gated on `target_has_atomic = "32"`;
417	  - the SPSC ring stays load/store-only (thumbv6m keeps
418	    working).
419	
420	### MPSC API: closure send
421	
422	The producer-side guard is replaced by a closure, because in
423	MPSC an abandoned *claim* wedges the queue — the consumer
424	waits at that position forever — where SPSC's dropped guard
425	was free (`producer_idx` never moved). Abandonment is made
426	unrepresentable rather than handled:
427	
428	- `MpscRing::init(region, slot_size, capacity)` /
429	  `unsafe attach` — mirror the SPSC constructors:
430	  - own magic and layout_version;
431	  - cross-attaching a region of the wrong kind fails
432	    toward `BadMagic`.
433	- `split() -> (MpscProducer, MpscConsumer)` with
434	  `MpscProducer: Clone + Send`:
435	  - one handle per producing thread;
436	  - cross-process producers attach;
437	  - the endpoint claims word models role slots, not a
438	    single producer bit, so N producer claims fit —
439	    already noted in its todo.
440	- `producer.send_with::<T>(on_full, fill)` →
441	  `Result<(), Full>`:
442	  - `fill: impl FnOnce(&mut T)` writes the payload in
443	    place — claim, fill, commit on closure return;
444	  - commit-by-construction — no public abandonment state;
445	  - `on_full(attempt) -> bool` is the same wait-policy
446	    seam as SPSC (`|_| false` = single probe).
447	- `consumer.reserve_slot_with::<T>(on_empty)` →
448	  `ReadSlot` — the SPSC consumer guard survives:
449	  - consumer abandonment is harmless (drop without
450	    release re-delivers the same slot);
451	  - so the guard shape stays.
452	- **Unwind path** — a panic in `fill` must not wedge the
453	  queue:
454	  - the unwind runs an internal guard's Drop, which
455	    tombstones the claimed slot — a marked commit the
456	    consumer releases without delivering;
457	  - sketch: a reserved seq bit masked out of the index
458	    arithmetic (constrains `M <= 2^30`); encoding is an
459	    open question below;
460	  - only unwinding reaches it — panic=abort targets never
461	    do.
462	
463	### Overflow readiness
464	
465	The sender-private overflow FIFO
466	([Overflow FIFO (future)](#overflow-fifo-future)) must
467	compose with MPSC when it lands. Two properties keep that
468	true; both are named requirements so a later protocol tweak
469	cannot silently break them:
470	
471	- **`Full` is a zero-footprint failure**:
472	  - a send returning `Full` leaves no claim, no tombstone,
473	    no protocol residue;
474	  - the protocol gives this by construction — fullness is
475	    observed in the slot seq *before* any CAS, and a lost
476	    CAS is also stateless — but it is a requirement, not
477	    an accident.
478	- **`Full` is the overflow seam**:
479	  - the future MPSC DescSender probes with `|_| false` and
480	    on `Err(Full)` appends the buffer to its own pending
481	    list;
482	  - pending lists stay per-sender — no shared list, no
483	    CAS;
484	  - per-sender draining preserves exactly the per-producer
485	    FIFO order MPSC promises anyway — inter-producer order
486	    was never promised.
487	
488	### MPSC trust
489	
490	Same posture as the SPSC ring: every shared word is
491	untrusted, corruption degrades service and never causes UB.
492	
493	- The seq array joins the untrusted set:
494	  - positions are always masked, so scribbled seqs yield
495	    spurious Full/Empty or garbage-but-valid `T`s
496	    (zerocopy guarantees representation, not sense);
497	  - never out-of-bounds access.
498	- A hostile *producer* peer is new surface relative to
499	  SPSC:
500	  - it can claim and never commit — wedging the queue, a
501	    liveness loss;
502	  - or commit garbage;
503	  - denial of service stays possible, as it always was —
504	    memory safety does not depend on peers.
505	
506	### Fan-in (composition, not a mode)
507	
508	The other standard many-to-one shape: one SPSC ring per
509	producer, the consumer polls N endpoints. Both have a place:
510	
511	- the shared MPSC ring:

file.content:
## MPSC ring (sibling primitive)

Design for the multi-producer single-consumer ring — a
sibling type next to the SPSC ring, not a mode of it. The
SPSC hot path is one Relaxed load + one Acquire load + one
Release store per side; any unified type would put at least a
per-slot sequence check into that path. So the primitives
stay separate, existing SPSC users pay nothing, and iiac-perf
measures the two directly against each other. Anticipated as
a one-bullet placeholder in
[Pool topology and phasing](#pool-topology-and-phasing); this
section is the full design.

### MPSC protocol

Vyukov's bounded MPMC queue restricted to many-producer /
one-consumer: producers CAS-claim `producer_idx`; a per-slot
sequence word publishes each slot independently. The seq
exists because claim order and commit order differ under
concurrency — the consumer must observe commits, not claims.

- **Sequence array** — `M × AtomicU32`, `seq[i]` initialized
  to `i`. Slot at free-running position `pos` (masked at
  access, as SPSC) is:
  - claimable when `seq == pos`,
  - committed when `seq == pos + 1`,
  - released by the consumer storing `seq = pos + M`.
- **Producer claim** — load `producer_idx` (Relaxed), load
  that slot's seq (Acquire), branch on the wrapping-signed
  diff `seq.wrapping_sub(pos) as i32`:
  - `== 0` — claimable:
    - `compare_exchange_weak(pos, pos + 1)` on
      `producer_idx`; success claims the slot exclusively.
    - Write the payload in place.
    - `seq.store(pos + 1, Release)` commits.
  - `< 0` — full:
    - The slot's previous occupant is not yet released.
    - The `on_full` policy decides retry vs give-up.
  - `> 0` — lost the race to another producer:
    - Reload `producer_idx` and retry.
    - Not a policy call: the system made progress, only
      this producer fell behind.
- **Consumer** — single, as in SPSC, so its side needs no
  CAS:
  - wait for `seq == c + 1` (Acquire),
  - read the slot,
  - store `seq = c + M` (Release),
  - advance the private index.
- **Producers never read `consumer_idx`**:
  - fullness falls out of the slot seq;
  - the header's consumer index line is diagnostic
    occupancy only.
- **Free-running u32 + power-of-two M** carry over
  unchanged: `2^32 % M == 0` keeps seq arithmetic and masked
  positions wrap-safe.
- **Atomic floor** — the claim CAS raises the floor for MPSC
  users only:
  - the module is gated on `target_has_atomic = "32"`;
  - the SPSC ring stays load/store-only (thumbv6m keeps
    working).

### MPSC API: closure send

The producer-side guard is replaced by a closure, because in
MPSC an abandoned *claim* wedges the queue — the consumer
waits at that position forever — where SPSC's dropped guard
was free (`producer_idx` never moved). Abandonment is made
unrepresentable rather than handled:

- `MpscRing::init(region, slot_size, capacity)` /
  `unsafe attach` — mirror the SPSC constructors:
  - own magic and layout_version;
  - cross-attaching a region of the wrong kind fails
    toward `BadMagic`.
- `split() -> (MpscProducer, MpscConsumer)` with
  `MpscProducer: Clone + Send`:
  - one handle per producing thread;
  - cross-process producers attach;
  - the endpoint claims word models role slots, not a
    single producer bit, so N producer claims fit —
    already noted in its todo.
- `producer.send_with::<T>(on_full, fill)` →
  `Result<(), Full>`:
  - `fill: impl FnOnce(&mut T)` writes the payload in
    place — claim, fill, commit on closure return;
  - commit-by-construction — no public abandonment state;
  - `on_full(attempt) -> bool` is the same wait-policy
    seam as SPSC (`|_| false` = single probe).
- `consumer.reserve_slot_with::<T>(on_empty)` →
  `ReadSlot` — the SPSC consumer guard survives:
  - consumer abandonment is harmless (drop without
    release re-delivers the same slot);
  - so the guard shape stays.
- **Unwind path** — a panic in `fill` must not wedge the
  queue:
  - the unwind runs an internal guard's Drop, which
    tombstones the claimed slot — a marked commit the
    consumer releases without delivering;
  - sketch: a reserved seq bit masked out of the index
    arithmetic (constrains `M <= 2^30`); encoding is an
    open question below;
  - only unwinding reaches it — panic=abort targets never
    do.

### Overflow readiness

The sender-private overflow FIFO
([Overflow FIFO (future)](#overflow-fifo-future)) must
compose with MPSC when it lands. Two properties keep that
true; both are named requirements so a later protocol tweak
cannot silently break them:

- **`Full` is a zero-footprint failure**:
  - a send returning `Full` leaves no claim, no tombstone,
    no protocol residue;
  - the protocol gives this by construction — fullness is
    observed in the slot seq *before* any CAS, and a lost
    CAS is also stateless — but it is a requirement, not
    an accident.
- **`Full` is the overflow seam**:
  - the future MPSC DescSender probes with `|_| false` and
    on `Err(Full)` appends the buffer to its own pending
    list;
  - pending lists stay per-sender — no shared list, no
    CAS;
  - per-sender draining preserves exactly the per-producer
    FIFO order MPSC promises anyway — inter-producer order
    was never promised.

### MPSC trust

Same posture as the SPSC ring: every shared word is
untrusted, corruption degrades service and never causes UB.

- The seq array joins the untrusted set:
  - positions are always masked, so scribbled seqs yield
    spurious Full/Empty or garbage-but-valid `T`s
    (zerocopy guarantees representation, not sense);
  - never out-of-bounds access.
- A hostile *producer* peer is new surface relative to
  SPSC:
  - it can claim and never commit — wedging the queue, a
    liveness loss;
  - or commit garbage;
  - denial of service stays possible, as it always was —
    memory safety does not depend on peers.

### Fan-in (composition, not a mode)

The other standard many-to-one shape: one SPSC ring per
producer, the consumer polls N endpoints. Both have a place:

- the shared MPSC ring:
  - arrival-order fairness ("fair FIFO");
  - one place to poll.
- fan-in:
  - stays load/store-only;
  - zero producer-to-producer interference;
  - the consumer chooses the service policy (priority,
    round-robin, weighted, …).

Fan-in is buildable today from shipped SPSC parts; likely
both shapes get offered eventually (a fan-in helper as a
later convenience — no commitment yet). The bench matrix
measures both from the start.

### MPSC measurement plan

iiac-perf (sibling repo) grows an mpsc set alongside `zcr`:

- `zcr-mpsc-1t` — same-thread round-trip: pure protocol
  overhead (CAS + seq vs load/store), against
  `zcr-with-1t`.
- `zcr-mpsc-2t` — 1 producer / 1 consumer cross-core: what
  MPSC costs when you don't need it, against `zcr-with-2t`.
- `zcr-mpsc-3t` / `-5t` — 2 / 4 producers, one consumer:
  claim-contention scaling, the numbers SPSC cannot
  produce.
- Fan-in comparator at the same producer counts (N SPSC
  rings, round-robin poll).
- We think the shared ring:
  - loses to fan-in on raw throughput under producer
    contention (CAS retries vs disjoint cache lines);
  - wins on consumer-side latency and fairness at larger
    N.
- The matrix exists to check that, not assume it.

### MPSC open questions

- **Seq array padding**:
  - packed `M × 4` bytes — Vyukov's shape; adjacent-slot
    false sharing;
  - line-padded — `M × 64` bytes of overhead;
  - start packed, and the bench matrix decides whether a
    padded variant earns a layout flag.
- **Tombstone encoding**:
  - reserved seq bit — masked arithmetic, `M <= 2^30`;
  - or a per-slot side word;
  - only the unwind path needs it, so the simplest
    correct encoding wins.
- **Header consumer index**:
  - keep as diagnostic occupancy (Relaxed stores by the
    consumer), or drop the line entirely;
  - the `occupancy()` polish todo argues for keeping it.

## Terminology

The vocabulary the rest of the document builds on, defined
once before first use:

- **slot** — a cache-line-aligned `[u8; N]`: one of the
  ring's M fixed-size buffers. Untyped storage — a message
  is a zerocopy `&T` / `&mut T` view into it (geometry
  details in [Constraints](#constraints)).
- **reserve** — take exclusive access to a slot through a
  guard:
  - the producer reserves the next free slot
    (`WriteSlot`);
  - the consumer reserves the oldest unread slot
    (`ReadSlot`).
- **commit** — the producer publishes its slot to the
  consumer; the handoff of ownership away from the writer:
  - SPSC: the `producer_idx` Release store;
  - MPSC: the slot's seq Release store.
- **release** — the consumer hands its slot back for
  reuse; the matching handoff in the other direction:
  - SPSC: the `consumer_idx` Release store;
  - MPSC: the slot's seq Release store (`pos + M`).
- **claim** — MPSC only: a producer wins exclusive
  ownership of a slot position by CAS on the shared
  producer index; writing and committing follow. Claim
  order and commit order can differ — see
  [MPSC ring (sibling primitive)](#mpsc-ring-sibling-primitive).

section that expands it.

    allocates, any holder frees.

  [details](#layer-requirements).
  - A process-private pool serves intra-process messaging.
- A message to be sent to another process must come from a
  pool in shared memory
  [details](#pool-id-resolution).
  - The receiving process must have that pool mapped.
- Queues carry descriptors, not payloads
  [details](#descriptors).
  - A descriptor is a uniform small
    `(pool id, buffer index)` regardless of message size.
  - The payload stays at rest in its pool buffer.
- Any message travels over any queue
  [details](#layer-requirements).
  - Precondition: both endpoints have the message's pool
    registered.
- Each process keeps its own pool registry
  [details](#descriptor-and-registry-design-070).
  - It maps pool ids to that process's view (mapping) of
    each pool.
  - Registries are private and never shared; only
    descriptors cross process boundaries.
  - Pool ids are the shared vocabulary: every participating
    process must register a pool under the same id (the
    assignment scheme is an open question —
    [details](#pool-id-allocation)).
- Ownership follows the descriptor
  [details](#usage-model-roles-and-buffer-lifecycle).
  - alloc → write → send the descriptor over a queue →
    the receiver owns it → read, forward over another
    queue, or free.
  - A buffer is always in exactly one state with one
    permitted toucher.
- Any holder — any thread or process — may free
  [details](#free-path-intrusive-lifo-free-stack).
  - The free pushes the buffer directly onto its pool's
    free-stack in shared memory — no round-trip through
    the owning process.
  - The owning allocator finds the buffer on a later
    alloc.

  memory are untrusted
  [details](#trust).

  [details](#descriptor-and-registry-design-070).

content.text:
Good instinct on both — let me map each against what's actually written.

## The two styles: already stated

The "single shared ring vs. N rings polled by one consumer" split, *and* the "no single winner, we'd have both" conclusion, are both explicit — in `### Fan-in (composition, not a mode)` (design doc line 506):

> "The other standard many-to-one shape: one SPSC ring per producer, the consumer polls N endpoints. **Both have a place**" ... "likely both shapes get offered eventually."

And it's not just asserted — the measurement plan (line 525) benches them head-to-head at matching producer counts and explicitly refuses to pick a winner up front: *"The matrix exists to check that, not assume it."* The current guess there is shared-ring loses on throughput under contention, wins on consumer-side latency/fairness at large N. So point 1 is captured, including your "probably both" hunch.

## The general-connectivity principle: only implicit

This one you're right to flag — it is **not** stated as a principle. What exists are three *facets* of it, scattered:

- **Consumer with in-degree > 1** — fan-in (one consumer, N rings). Covered.
- **A node that both receives and sends** — System model line 621: *"alloc → write → send the descriptor over a queue → the receiver owns it → read, **forward over another queue**, or free."* That's one entity holding a consume endpoint and a produce endpoint — a 2-hop pipeline — but stated as a lifecycle step, not a topology rule.
- **Message mobility** — line 605: *"Any message travels over any queue."*

What's missing is the step back that ties them together: **the ring/queue is an *edge*; an entity is a *node* that may hold any number of endpoints, in either role, at once** — consume ring A while producing on B and C. Connections form an arbitrary directed graph, not just 1:1 links or many-to-one stars. And note one corner isn't even implied: **fan-out from a single producer** (one entity, multiple outbound edges) appears nowhere — every current shape is many-to-*one*.

So: the two edge-flavors are well-covered; the node-degree / graph framing above them is the gap.

## Proposed statement

I'd add it as a top-level principle bullet in `### System model (overview)` (the "rules at a glance" list is exactly the right altitude), something like:

> - **Entities form an arbitrary directed graph** [details](#…).
>   - A queue/ring is an *edge*; an entity (thread or process) is a *node* that may hold any number of endpoints, in either role, at once — consume ring A while producing on B and C.
>   - The two many-to-one realizations (shared MPSC ring, fan-in of SPSC rings) are two edge-shapes for the same consumer in-degree; neither is privileged.
>   - Fan-out (one producer, many edges) is equally admissible — nothing in the primitives assumes an entity has degree 1.

That also feeds the chores-02 refactoring: if `spsc`/`mpsc` are sibling *edge* primitives and composition (fan-in/fan-out) is a node-level concern, it reinforces keeping the two ring flavors as peer modules and leaving composition helpers out of them.

Want me to add that bullet to the System model overview (and maybe a one-line echo in the ring-buffer `## Goal` so the graph framing is visible up top)? Or would you rather it live somewhere else — a short dedicated `### Topology` subsection?

Yes — we agree on the substance, and let me state it precisely so there's no daylight between us:

**A single thread can simultaneously hold:**
- `Producer`/`Consumer` handles for any number of SPSC rings,
- `MpscProducer`/`MpscConsumer` handles for any number of MPSC rings,
- the owning-allocator role for several pools, and free into any pool it can reach.

Nothing caps the degree. These handles are just owned values — a thread holds as many as it likes; the endpoint and pool contracts constrain *how each single ring/pool is used* (one producer per SPSC ring, one allocator per pool), never *how many* a thread participates in. So a thread is a hub, not a leaf.

**On the granularity you flagged** — you're right that the bullet pins "entity" to thread/process, and I think that pinning is *correct but incomplete*, for a reason worth being explicit about:

- Thread/process is the **contract** granularity — it's the level at which the guarantees are actually defined: `Send`/`Sync` bounds, per-process shared-memory mapping, "single producer" = one thread driving an SPSC endpoint at a time. So for ownership and safety, thread/process is the right node.
- But the **logical** communicating unit can be *finer* than a thread — an actor/task on a shared executor. "One thread holding N edges" and "N actors sharing one thread, each holding one edge" are indistinguishable at the wiring level. So thread/process is the *realization* granularity, not a claim that a node = exactly one logical role.

Put differently: the design neither requires nor forbids one-endpoint-per-thread. A thread multiplexing many logical roles is exactly the multiplicity you're describing.

**Two small gaps this exposes in the written doc:**
1. The new bullet says "endpoints" and only illustrates rings — it doesn't mention pools. Your statement lumps pools in.
2. Pool multiplicity is stated at *process* granularity ("A process may have one or more pools") but thread→multiple-pools isn't explicit.

>   - A queue is an edge; an entity — a thread or process, possibly hosting several logical roles — is a node that may hold endpoints for many rings at once, in either role, and own or free into several pools: consume ring A, produce on B and C, allocate from pool P.

content.text:
Grepped — ISRs are absent from the whole design corpus. The only hit is line 309, "may WFE on an interrupt," which is the *blocking* layer (a thread parking until an interrupt wakes it), not an ISR *holding an endpoint*. So this is genuinely new ground, and you're correcting a real flattening in my model.

## The corrected model: two axes, not one list

I wrongly put "thread" and "process" in one bag ("the concrete unit"). They're different axes:

- **Execution context** — the thing that actually runs code and drives ring/pool ops. Two kinds, and you're right they're distinct: **thread** and **ISR**. An ISR isn't a thread — it preempts, runs to completion, and can't block. *This* is the execution granularity, and it's what a graph node really is.
- **Process** — not an executor at all. It's an address space grouping one or more execution contexts, and it's the boundary that owns the shared-memory mappings and pool registries. It's a *fence around* nodes, not a node.

So: **nodes are execution contexts (threads/ISRs); rings are edges; a process is a boundary the graph spans.** Crossing a process edge = crossing into another address space (needs shared memory + a registered pool). My "concrete unit" line conflated the executor with the fence.

## Why this matters here (not just pedantry)

ISR-as-endpoint is a first-class case for *this* library specifically, and it has teeth:

- **Lock-free is exactly what makes ISR endpoints viable** — SPSC's load/store (and MPSC's CAS) hold no lock across any point, so an ISR can produce/consume without the classic "ISR grabbed a mutex the thread holds" deadlock.
- **The non-blocking probe is the ISR wait policy** — an ISR must not spin unbounded, so `on_full`/`on_empty` = `|_| false` (single probe, bail on Full/Empty) is precisely the ISR-appropriate policy. The wait-policy seam already accommodates it.
- **"Single producer" must mean single *context*, and preemption sharpens it** — if a thread *and* an ISR both produce on one SPSC ring, that's two producers; worse, the ISR preempts the thread mid-`reserve`/`commit` on the same core → protocol violation. I'm confident the fix is: thread + ISR feeding one ring ⇒ use MPSC, whose CAS-claim tolerates the preemptive interleave (the ISR just claims a different slot; the thread resumes and commits). That's a genuinely useful rule the doc doesn't state.

## Proposed bullet (revised)

> - **Entities form an arbitrary directed graph** [details](#fan-in-composition-not-a-mode).
>   - A ring is an edge; a node is an *execution context* — a thread or an ISR — that may hold endpoints for many rings at once, in either role, and own or free into several pools: consume ring A, produce on B and C, allocate from pool P.
>   - A *process* is not a node but a boundary — an address space grouping one or more execution contexts and owning the pool mappings and registries; an edge crossing it crosses into another address space (shared memory + a registered pool).
>   - The two many-to-one realizations (shared MPSC ring, fan-in of SPSC rings) are two edge-shapes for the same consumer in-degree; neither is privileged.
>   - Fan-out (one producer, many edges) is equally admissible — nothing in the primitives assumes a node has degree 1.

Two questions before I write it in:

1. Does that two-axis framing (node = thread/ISR execution context; process = boundary) match how you see it?
2. The "single producer = single context; thread + ISR ⇒ MPSC" rule feels worth capturing too, but it's a correctness note, not a topology-overview line. Want it as a separate short note (a `### Execution contexts` subsection, or a Constraints bullet), or leave it out of the doc for now?

file.content:
## Constraints

- **Slot geometry: M slots × N bytes** — the ring holds M
  [slots](#terminology) of N bytes each.
  - M (capacity) is a power of two — index wrapping is a
    mask, no division; checked at construction.
  - N (slot size) is a multiple of the cache-line size, and
    each slot is cache-line aligned — adjacent slots never
    share a line, and any `T` with `align_of::<T>()` up to
    the line size fits without padding logic.
  - This trades space for isolation (a 16-byte message
    occupies a full 64-byte slot) — the right default for
    IPC; a packed-slot variant is a possible follow-on.
- **Monotonic indices** — producer/consumer indices are
  free-running unsigned counters (wrap via masking), so
  full/empty are distinguishable without a separate flag or
  a sacrificial slot.
- **Cache-line separation** — producer- and consumer-owned
  indices are padded/aligned to separate cache lines to avoid
  false sharing.
- **Typed access over untyped slots** — the slot geometry
  (M, N) is a property of the buffer, independent of any
  message type; a `T` is a zerocopy view into a slot, valid
  when `size_of::<T>() <= N` and `align_of::<T>()` divides
  the slot alignment (checked at construction or compile
  time). Variable-length framed messages are a possible
  follow-on, not in this cycle.
- **zerocopy, core-only** — depend on zerocopy with default
  features compatible with `no_std`; no other runtime
  dependencies.

## Memory layout

content.text:
You're right — my bullet overstated it. The SPSC contract isn't about *thread identity*; it's about **no concurrent access to the endpoint**. A mutex serializes access, so any number of threads can share the single-producer role by taking turns — at each instant only the lock-holder is in the producer path, which is exactly the invariant SPSC needs. And it's Rust-expressible: `Producer: Send`, so `Arc<Mutex<Producer>>` shares fine; the guard hands you the `&mut Producer` to `reserve`/`commit`.

So the real constraint is *serialization*, not "one thread." Where your mutex intuition **does** hit a wall is the ISR case, and for a specific reason: an ISR can't take that mutex. It preempts the thread that holds the lock and can't block waiting for it (deadlock / priority inversion). So ISR+thread on one endpoint is genuinely concurrent, unserializable-by-lock access — that's the case that forces lock-free MPSC. Two different reasons collapsed into my one bad sentence.

Corrected bullet — I've swapped it in:

> - **SPSC roles forbid concurrent access, not multiple owners** — the single-producer (and single-consumer) contract is "at most one accessor in the endpoint at a time," a *serialization* requirement, not a fixed thread identity.
>   - One thread trivially satisfies it; so do several threads taking turns under a mutex — the lock guarantees no two are in the producer path at once, at the cost of blocking (no longer lock-free).
>   - An ISR is the case a lock cannot cover: it preempts and must not block on a thread-held lock (deadlock / priority inversion), so an ISR sharing a role with a thread is genuine concurrent access — a violation.
>   - Need truly concurrent producers, or a thread + ISR on one ring? Use the MPSC ring — its lock-free CAS claim serializes without a lock.

Applied. One ripple to flag: the **overview** bullet still says "a node is an execution context — a thread or an ISR — that may hold endpoints." content.text:
Agreed — all of it. Let me state the mechanics precisely so we're locked:

- **Consumer is symmetric** — yes, a mutex shares the single-consumer role across threads exactly as it does the producer. My wording was producer-flavored; fixing that.
- **ISRs *can* share, via try-and-bail** — right: an ISR may participate only if it **tests** the flag and, if taken, **abandons** (defers/drops) rather than spinning. Never block, never wait.
- **The real enabler is interrupt-disable** — this is the crux. A same-core ISR can't be serialized by a lock (it can't wait on it), so the *only* way to make the thread's multi-step endpoint access atomic against that ISR is for the thread to run it with interrupts disabled — the `spin_lock_irqsave` pattern. The mutex/flag handles thread-vs-thread; the irq-disable handles thread-vs-ISR. Together they cover both.
- **So the default rule stands** — an ISR *should* be the sole owner of any endpoint it touches; sharing is possible but is the "special care" path, and it hinges on the thread being able to disable interrupts.

That also corrects my previous bullet, which called thread+ISR sharing a flat "violation" — it's a violation *only without* the irqsave guard. Reworked and swapped in:

- **zerocopy, core-only** — depend on zerocopy with default
  features compatible with `no_std`; no other runtime
  dependencies.

### Execution contexts

A graph node is an *execution context* — a thread or an ISR
that drives endpoints (a process is only the address-space
boundary around one or more of them,
[System model](#system-model-overview)). Each satisfies the
serialization rule above — one accessor in an endpoint at a
time — differently:

- **Thread, sole owner** — one thread owns the role; no
  sharing, fully lock-free.
- **Threads sharing a role** — several take turns under a
  mutex:
  - producer and consumer sides are symmetric;
  - the lock keeps two out of the endpoint at once;
  - cost is blocking — the role is no longer lock-free.
- **ISR, sole owner** — the default, because an ISR cannot be
  serialized by a plain lock:
  - it preempts and must not block on a thread-held one
    (deadlock / priority inversion);
  - so an ISR is the sole owner of any endpoint it touches.
- **ISR sharing a role with a thread** — possible, but the
  "special care" path:
  - the thread guards its access with interrupts disabled
    (irqsave — the only way to be atomic against a same-core
    ISR);
  - the ISR must try-and-bail: test the access flag, and if
    taken, abandon rather than spin;
  - it hinges on the thread being able to disable interrupts.
- **Lock-free concurrency instead** — many producers, or a
  thread + ISR without the irq-disable dance:
  - use the MPSC ring; its CAS claim serializes without a
    lock.

content.text:
On **line 136** I think we've got a terminology mismatch, not a technical one — let me make my case before I touch it.

"Sole owner" there means the ISR exclusively owns **one endpoint**, *not* the whole ring. A ring is two endpoints; "ISR sole owner" is the mirror of "Thread sole owner" one bullet up — it's about a single endpoint having a single, unshared owner. The **partner is the other endpoint**, owned by someone else.

So the canonical embedded case *is* exactly this, and it needs **no** interrupt-disable:

- ISR owns the **producer** endpoint (sole owner), a thread owns the **consumer** endpoint (sole owner). Real partners — just on opposite ends of the ring.
- No irqsave required, because producer and consumer touch **disjoint** state: the producer writes `producer_idx` + the slot it's filling; the consumer writes `consumer_idx` + the slot it's reading; the cross-reads (full/empty checks) are single atomic loads. The ISR preempting its partner thread mid-op is just one more interleaving the lock-free SPSC handoff already tolerates (the same one it tolerates across two cores).
- Interrupt-disable only enters when the ISR **shares one endpoint** with a thread — i.e. both produce on the same ring. That's the *next* bullet ("ISR sharing a role with a thread"), and there it's genuinely required.

So your instinct "a ring without a partner is useless" is right — but "ISR sole owner" never meant a partnerless ring; it meant the ISR doesn't *share its end*. The partner sits on the other end.

Where I think the bullet failed you is that "sole owner" reads as "owns the ring alone." Proposed rewrite to kill that:

> - **ISR, sole owner of an endpoint** — the canonical embedded ring: the ISR exclusively owns *one* end (say the producer), a thread owns the other.
>   - No interrupt disable needed — producer and consumer touch disjoint state, so the ISR preempting its partner is just another interleaving the lock-free handoff tolerates.
>   - Why *sole* ownership: an ISR can't be serialized by a lock (it preempts, can't block on a thread-held one), so it must never *share* an endpoint — only own one outright.

Does that reasoning land? If so I'll swap it in; if you're picturing a case where the ISR-owned endpoint really does need interrupt-disable, walk me through it — I may be missing your scenario.

Agreed — and that's the precise precondition, better than my loose "disjoint state." What makes preemption safe isn't that the two sides touch unrelated memory (they share the header and slots), it's that within those common structures:

1. **Single writer per field** — a field the ISR writes (its index, its in-flight slot) is read-only to the partner; no field has two writers.
2. **Atomic access** — the shared control words are read/written atomically, so no torn value crosses the preemption point.

That's exactly what the SPSC protocol already enforces (`producer_idx` written only by the producer, `consumer_idx` only by the consumer, both `AtomicU32`; the slot payload single-writer and published through the index handoff). So the "no irqsave" claim *depends* on those two conditions holding — which is worth saying, not assuming.

Consistency done in the subsection (both headers now "…of an endpoint" / "…an endpoint", and "role"→"endpoint" throughout). One holdover: the **Constraints bullet title** still says "SPSC **roles** forbid concurrent access" — that's the *category* usage ("the producer/consumer roles"), which reads fine, but say the word and I'll make it "endpoints" too.

## The all-ISR system: yes, and it's arguably the sweet spot

Short answer: **yes** — N producing ISRs → 1 consuming ISR works today on a single MPSC ring, and N:M works as composition. And it's not a stretch of the design; lock-free rings are *exactly* what ISR-to-ISR wants. Here's the reasoning and the real constraints.

**Why it works at all** — the ring protocols never block and never hold a lock:
- SPSC is load/store, MPSC is CAS. An ISR can execute either — bounded, non-blocking work.
- **No priority inversion is even possible** — there's no lock for a low-priority ISR to hold hostage, so a high-priority ISR never waits on a low one. That's the failure mode that normally makes ISR IPC scary, and it's structurally absent here. Big win.
- Nested-ISR preemption is just another interleaving the protocol tolerates: a higher-priority producing ISR preempts a lower one mid-fill → it claims a *different* slot (MPSC `producer_idx` already advanced), commits; the consumer waits in-order at the lower slot until the preempted ISR resumes and commits. No corruption.

**N producing ISRs → 1 consuming ISR** — a single shared MPSC ring:
- Producers CAS-claim, fill, commit — with `on_full = |_| false` so a full ring makes the ISR **bail**, never spin.
- The consuming ISR drains with `on_empty = |_| false`, bailing when empty.
- The tombstone/unwind path is irrelevant — ISRs run panic=abort, so a fill never unwinds.

**N:M (fan-out / consolidation)** — there is no N:M *ring*; it's a **graph**, which is exactly the topology we just documented:
- Consolidate (N→1): shared MPSC ring, or fan-in of N SPSC rings.
- Fan-out (1→M): one producer, M ring edges (the fan-out case we just added).
- N:M: wire the two — N producers into a consolidation node, then fan out to M consumers, or a routing node in between. All nodes can be ISRs.

**The constraints that come with "all-ISR"** (these are the design teeth):
1. **Non-blocking only** — ISRs must use bail-immediately policies. No `spin`. So backpressure can't be "wait" — it must be **drop, or overflow-FIFO** (todo #2). This makes the overflow work *more* central for an ISR system than a thread one.
2. **Doorbell / trigger** — a consuming ISR only runs when *something fires it*. In an all-ISR system a producer typically has to raise a software interrupt / IPI to run the consumer after committing. The ring doesn't provide that; it's a system-level trigger (the header's user-line "blocking contract" is the natural seam, but for ISRs it's a soft-IRQ trigger, not a futex).
3. **Atomics floor splits the two styles** — the shared MPSC ring needs CAS (`target_has_atomic = "32"`, ≥ Cortex-M3). On a **Cortex-M0 / thumbv6m (no CAS)**, N producers can't share an MPSC ring — you *must* use fan-in (N SPSC rings, load/store only). This is a concrete reason both many-to-one styles have to exist, now with a hard target-driven answer.
4. **CAS retry latency** under heavy nested-ISR contention is bounded but not constant — usually fine, worth noting for hard-real-time budgets.

So: possible, and it lands cleanly on primitives you already have — the new part is naming the *system-level* pieces (non-blocking backpressure, the doorbell, the CAS-vs-fan-in target split).

## Recording it as a design goal

This is a real new goal, not a wording tweak, so I don't want to just drop it into `## Goal` unilaterally. My suggestion:

- Add a line to **`## Goal`** naming ISR-to-ISR (all-ISR) messaging as an explicit target,
- and a **`### ISR-only systems`** subsection (near Execution contexts, or under it) capturing the four constraints above + the N:1 / N:M composition, with a todo pointer for the doorbell/backpressure pieces that aren't built yet.

Before I write: is that the right home and scope? And do you want the doorbell/trigger + non-blocking-backpressure treated as *in-scope for this goal* (so they become todo items), or just noted as prerequisites? Tell me the framing and I'll draft it.

1	# Ring buffer design
2	
3	Design notes for the zero-copy ring buffer. This file uses
4	[Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It covers terminology,
5	requirements, constraints, the memory layout, the API, and
6	the validation ladder, and is kept in sync with the
7	implementation in `src/` (as-built): `lib.rs` holds the
8	region-level machinery (header, init/attach/split, geometry
9	helpers) and re-exports the endpoint modules `producer.rs`
10	(Producer, WriteSlot, Full) and `consumer.rs` (Consumer,
11	ReadSlot, Empty).
12	
13	## Goal
14	
15	A ring buffer for passing typed messages between a producer and
16	a consumer with no copying at the boundary: the producer writes
17	its message directly into buffer memory, the consumer reads it
18	in place. The same layout must work intra-process (threads) and
19	inter-process (shared memory).
20	
21	## Terminology
22	
23	The vocabulary the rest of the document builds on, defined
24	once before first use:
25	
26	- **slot** — a cache-line-aligned `[u8; N]`: one of the
27	  ring's M fixed-size buffers. Untyped storage — a message
28	  is a zerocopy `&T` / `&mut T` view into it (geometry
29	  details in [Constraints](#constraints)).
30	- **reserve** — take exclusive access to a slot through a
31	  guard:
32	  - the producer reserves the next free slot
33	    (`WriteSlot`);
34	  - the consumer reserves the oldest unread slot
35	    (`ReadSlot`).
36	- **commit** — the producer publishes its slot to the
37	  consumer; the handoff of ownership away from the writer:
38	  - SPSC: the `producer_idx` Release store;
39	  - MPSC: the slot's seq Release store.
40	- **release** — the consumer hands its slot back for
41	  reuse; the matching handoff in the other direction:
42	  - SPSC: the `consumer_idx` Release store;
43	  - MPSC: the slot's seq Release store (`pos + M`).
44	- **claim** — MPSC only: a producer wins exclusive
45	  ownership of a slot position by CAS on the shared
46	  producer index; writing and committing follow. Claim
47	  order and commit order can differ — see
48	  [MPSC ring (sibling primitive)](#mpsc-ring-sibling-primitive).
49	
50	## Requirements

Design notes for the zero-copy ring buffer. This file uses
[Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It covers terminology,
requirements, constraints, the memory layout, the API, and
the validation ladder, and is kept in sync with the
implementation in `src/` (as-built): `lib.rs` holds the
region-level machinery (header, init/attach/split, geometry
helpers) and re-exports the endpoint modules `producer.rs`
(Producer, WriteSlot, Full) and `consumer.rs` (Consumer,
ReadSlot, Empty).

## Goal

A ring buffer for passing typed messages between a producer and
a consumer with no copying at the boundary: the producer writes
its message directly into buffer memory, the consumer reads it
in place. The same layout must work intra-process (threads) and
inter-process (shared memory).

## Terminology

The vocabulary the rest of the document builds on, defined
once before first use:

- **slot** — a cache-line-aligned `[u8; N]`: one of the
  ring's M fixed-size buffers. Untyped storage — a message
  is a zerocopy `&T` / `&mut T` view into it (geometry
  details in [Constraints](#constraints)).
- **reserve** — take exclusive access to a slot through a
  guard:
  - the producer reserves the next free slot
    (`WriteSlot`);
  - the consumer reserves the oldest unread slot
    (`ReadSlot`).
- **commit** — the producer publishes its slot to the
  consumer; the handoff of ownership away from the writer:
  - SPSC: the `producer_idx` Release store;
  - MPSC: the slot's seq Release store.
- **release** — the consumer hands its slot back for
  reuse; the matching handoff in the other direction:
  - SPSC: the `consumer_idx` Release store;
  - MPSC: the slot's seq Release store (`pos + M`).
- **claim** — MPSC only: a producer wins exclusive
  ownership of a slot position by CAS on the shared
  producer index; writing and committing follow. Claim
  order and commit order can differ — see
  [MPSC ring (sibling primitive)](#mpsc-ring-sibling-primitive).

## Requirements

input.new_string:
A ring buffer for passing typed messages between a producer and
a consumer with no copying at the boundary: the producer writes
its message directly into buffer memory, the consumer reads it
in place. The same layout must work intra-process (threads) and
inter-process (shared memory).

**Blue-sky goal: ISR-to-ISR messaging.** A stretch target —
the same rings carrying messages between *interrupt service
routines*, no thread in the loop: N producing ISRs into one
consuming ISR, up to N:M for fan-out / consolidation (entities
as a graph of ISR nodes,
[Execution contexts](#execution-contexts)). Lock-free rings
suit ISRs — no lock means no priority inversion — but the
gotchas are real:

- **No blocking, ever** — an ISR bails, never waits, on Full
  or Empty (the `|_| false` policy); backpressure must be drop
  or an overflow FIFO, never a spin.
- **Something must fire the consumer** — a consuming ISR runs
  only when triggered, so an all-ISR system needs a doorbell
  (a software interrupt / IPI raised after commit); the ring
  does not provide it.
- **The CAS floor splits the styles by target** — a shared
  MPSC ring needs `target_has_atomic = "32"` (≥ Cortex-M3);
  on a no-CAS M0 / thumbv6m, N producers must fan in over N
  SPSC rings (load/store only) instead.
- **Latency is bounded, not constant** — MPSC CAS retries
  under nested-ISR contention are bounded, but a hard
  real-time budget must account for them.
- **panic=abort assumed** — the tombstone/unwind path never
  runs in an ISR, so a panicking fill aborts rather than
  degrading; fine, but a property to design around.

That's the same sensor — Adafruit 4754 is the **BNO085 9-DOF breakout** (BNO085/BNO080, STEMMA QT). The "Arm Cortex-M0" on it is exactly the internal fusion hub I flagged: closed SH-2 firmware, **not user-programmable**. You talk to it over I2C/SPI/UART; you can't load our message system onto its M0. So as a *host* target, this one's a no-go — it's a sensor, not a board.

But it pairs perfectly with a real M0+ host, and those are what you actually want for the thumbv6m/fan-in validation:

- **RP2040 boards** — Adafruit Feather RP2040, QT Py RP2040. Dual **Cortex-M0+** (ARMv6-M): **no LDREX/STREX → no native CAS**, Rust target `thumbv6m-none-eabi`. That's precisely the "no shared MPSC, must fan in" branch — *and* it's dual-core, so you can exercise true cross-core SPSC too.
- **SAMD21 boards** — Feather M0, QT Py SAMD21, Trinket M0. Single Cortex-M0+, same no-CAS thumbv6m story.

Both have STEMMA QT, so this exact BNO085 breakout plugs straight in. And it gives you a genuinely on-point test of the blue-sky goal: the BNO085's **data-ready interrupt** fires an ISR on the M0+ host, that ISR reads the sample over I2C and **produces into a ring**, a consumer drains it — an ISR-producer on a no-CAS target, validating the fan-in path end-to-end.

content.text:
Yes — the ESP32 is the one thing in that setup you *can* program (unlike the BNO085's locked M0). But it validates a *different* branch than the M0 does, so it's worth being precise about what it buys you:

**What the ESP32 is:** dual-core **Xtensa LX6** (not ARM). Crucially, it **has** atomic compare-swap (the `S32C1I` instruction), so `target_has_atomic = "32"` is true — it runs the **shared MPSC ring** natively. Programmable in Rust via esp-rs / `esp-hal` (no_std); the Xtensa core needs the `espup` toolchain (the RISC-V ESP32 variants — C3/C6 — use upstream Rust instead).

**What it validates (a lot):**
- ✅ The message system running on a real MCU, no_std.
- ✅ **Cross-core** SPSC/MPSC — it's genuinely dual-core, so two cores hand off through a ring, not just preemptive threads.
- ✅ **ISR-producer** — the BNO085's INT pin → an ESP32 GPIO ISR → read the sample over I2C → produce into a ring. That's a direct chunk of the blue-sky ISR goal, on real hardware.

**What it does *not* validate:**
- ❌ The **no-CAS fan-in branch**. The ESP32 *has* atomics, so it takes the MPSC path — the same path a desktop takes. The whole reason the M0 was interesting is that it *lacks* CAS and forces fan-in. The ESP32 skips that fork entirely.

So they're complementary: **ESP32 = fastest path to "does it run, cross-core, ISR-producer" on hardware**; **M0+ (RP2040/SAMD21) = the specific proof that the fan-in fallback works where CAS doesn't exist.** The BNO085 breakout plugs into either over I2C.

Given two real targets now, want me to add a single `## Ideas` entry to `notes/todo.md` capturing both — e.g. *"hardware bring-up: run the ring on ESP32 (cross-core + BNO085 INT→ISR producer, has-CAS/MPSC) and on an M0+ host (RP2040/SAMD21, no-CAS/fan-in), sensor over I2C"*? That keeps the has-CAS and no-CAS validation paths recorded without touching the design doc.

Back to our docs.
"**ISR, sole ower of an endpoint**" Can the ISR code be the consumer and multiple threads can be the producer and our current MPSC works without interrupt disable but if we use EI/DI then an ISR can be the consumer and multiple ISRs and threads could be producers?

content.text:
Great question — and the answer splits cleanly, but not along the axis you'd expect. The thing that decides whether you need EI/DI is **whether the hardware has CAS**, *not* whether a producer is an ISR.

## Q1: ISR consumer + N thread producers — yes, no EI/DI

The current MPSC handles this as-is. The consumer is the **sole owner of the consumer endpoint**, and it works because:

- The consumer **never touches `producer_idx`** — it reads `seq[c]` (atomic, Acquire), reads the slot, then releases `seq[c] = c+M` and advances its **private** `consumer_idx`.
- Every shared word is single-writer + atomic: `consumer_idx` (consumer only), `producer_idx` (producers only, via CAS), `seq[i]` (producer-commit then consumer-release, phase-separated, never concurrent on one slot).
- An ISR consumer firing mid-way through a producer thread's claim only ever drains **committed** slots; the slot the producer is still filling has `seq != c+1`, so the in-order consumer stops there. Disjoint. No interrupt disable.

This is exactly your "ISR, sole owner of an endpoint" bullet — just with the ISR on the *consumer* side and MPSC on the producer side.

## Q2: add ISR producers — EI/DI only matters on no-CAS hardware

Here's the reframe. What multiple producers need is an **atomic claim** (the RMW on `producer_idx`). Who provides that atomicity depends on the target:

- **Has-CAS target (Cortex-M3+, ESP32, desktop):** you do **not** need EI/DI, even to add ISR producers. The current MPSC already supports **N producers (threads *and* ISRs) → 1 consumer (thread *or* ISR)** with no interrupt disable. The CAS is LL/SC (LDREX/STREX): if an ISR preempts a producer mid-claim, the STREX fails and it retries; if the producer already won and is filling, the ISR claims a *different* slot. ISRs run to completion, so they commit promptly. It just works.
- **No-CAS target (Cortex-M0 / thumbv6m):** there's no hardware CAS for the claim. **This** is where EI/DI earns its keep — disable interrupts around the `producer_idx` read-modify-write to emulate the atomic claim, and then yes, ISRs + threads can all be producers on the shared MPSC ring.

So your instinct is right, but the trigger is the missing CAS, not the ISR. EI/DI is a **substitute for a CAS the M0 doesn't have** — it's the same thing hardware CAS does on bigger cores.

Two caveats on the M0 EI/DI path:
- It **lengthens interrupt latency** — the claim blocks *all* interrupts (including the consumer ISR) for those few instructions. Keep the critical section tiny.
- It's **single-core only**. On a dual-core no-CAS part (RP2040), disabling interrupts on one core doesn't stop the other — you'd also need a hardware spinlock (the RP2040's SIO locks, which is how `portable-atomic` emulates CAS there).

And the alternative to EI/DI on M0 is the one already in the doc: **fan-in** — give each producer its own SPSC ring (no shared claim, no CAS, no EI/DI at all). So on no-CAS hardware the real design fork is **EI/DI-emulated shared MPSC** vs **fan-in of SPSC rings**.

That last fork (EI/DI-emulated CAS as a *third* option beside "shared MPSC" and "fan-in") isn't in the doc yet — the blue-sky gotcha currently jumps straight from "no CAS" to "fan-in." Want me to add EI/DI-emulated-claim as the middle option there, or keep it in conversation until you've actually tried it on the M0+?
